speaker: interviewee
text: --running a textile mill.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I kind of took the tragedy of having lost my parents, and my youngest siblings, and older siblings, tragically. I couldn't stop crying after the war. I just didn't care. I wanted my mother. And I wanted my mother. And I felt I wanted to be with her. And I didn't care who heard me cry. It was going on-- I just-- especially, during the holidays. I just didn't feel I wanted to go on living. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But somehow, the survival instinct took over, and we were thinking-- we began to think of our future. My brother took a position as a manager of a textile place. And we were-- he moved out to a private apartment. And my sisters and I and another brother stayed on DP camp. And we decided to take advantage of the opportunities of education by the ORT, where my sister learned to sew. And I learned to be a dental technician. I have a diploma from [NON-ENGLISH]. And later on, I thought I wanted to become a dentist. And I went to watch a movie-- watch-- being performed-- a surgery being performed in the mouth, in Munich. And my brother, at that time, was studying dentistry-- brother Nathan. And I fainted. And my sister was waiting outside. I-- I forced myself, because there was a-- a friend of mine who was studying dental technology with me together. And he was there. And just to show him that I am strong enough, I kept on watching the surgery being performed, even though I couldn't. And I fainted. So I decided at that stage-- I said, well-- I said to my sister, I am not a man. I'm not-- [LAUGHS] I will give up. And prior to that, I started getting ready for matriculation in order to be admitted to the university. So I had a lot of private tutors come to the house, because we had an income. My brothers and sister kind of took over the parental role. So we were kind of back being sort of a family. My sister, Esther, went to work. We had our own store in Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Textiles also, yes. And well, the younger ones studied, like my brother Nathan and I, because the German authorities wrote letters to us to go to school. And I couldn't see myself going to school together with German kids. And that's why, you know, I had private tutors. A cousin of mine did go to school with German kids. Now I also studied languages, because we made the decision-- the-- at first we wanted to migrate to Israel. But at that time, they were sending the immigrants to Cyprus. And later on, we heard from boys who left for Israel that they would take them to the front, and some of them died. And-- and since my brothers were of an age where we were sure-- though, we decided at that stage, to wait and not to emigrate yet. And somehow time went on. And we decided-- we-- we established contact with our relatives in America. And they sent us affidavits to go to America. But the Korean War was raging at the time. And a friend of my brother's was killed in the Korean War-- a brother, Simon.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we decided that we had enough of wars. And we just wanted to save the remnants of the family. And we decided to be far away from wars-- to emigrate to Australia. And what year did you do this?

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, when you got to Australia, did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: As we all-- when you arrive in a new country, you have to work. We didn't have any other means of support. We had no capital. We-- my sisters went to work in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther, and Manya-- and Mary-- sewing. And they started taking home sewing. And I succeeded in getting job at the Australian National Airways. Since I studied languages, I could converse quite well in English. I also studied French in Germany. So I thought that French might come in handy as a translator. And I worked for the Australian National Airways. But prior to that, on the boat to Australia, a man from the Joint Distribution Committee came-- by the name of Brent-- he came to greet us Jewish people-- new immigrants-- to Australia. And since he couldn't speak German, somehow they found out that I speak English, so they selected me to be the interpreter for the Jewish immigrants on the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you able to start a family after you got to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- my mind was not on starting a family. Actually, none of us were thinking of-- we were so closely knitted. And my older sister was becoming, you know, aware of time to start a family. And she left Australia. And she went to America. And then my brother and I decided to take a world tour. We were quite successful in Australia. My sisters and brothers and I worked together. We actually established a blouse factory in Australia. So I worked in the office. And the rest did the sewing and managing. My brothers were the managers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So back in-- in-- my sister was the first one to get married in America. We couldn't come to the wedding. It was 1954. And a couple of years later, my brother Nathan and I decided we want to take a trip to visit Israel and to see my sister in America. And so you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I actually, we intended going back to America-- to Australia. But somehow we decided to stay. We had to leave in order to get working permits. We had to go to Niagara Falls, to the Canadian side. And we were given permits to work, because we were Australian citizens at the time. And I worked at the-- for Dell Publishing Company, as an accountant, bookkeeping. I was in charge of subsidiary ledger. And my brother couldn't practice dentistry, since he didn't have the credentials in the United States. He took a temporary job where he worked as a-- he visited or worked a dental technician, where he met a man by the name of Steve Roisman. And they became friends. And as it turned out, Steve Roisman became my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married in '59, in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: On what day?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was June the 13th-- Saturday, June the 13th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two. I have one only left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what were your children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen Jacqeuline. Helen after my mother, Hinda, and Rosalyn after my little sister, Roizula. And I still call her Roizula, my Roizula.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one granddaughter, Nicole.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of activities are you engaged in these days to keep you busy?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- I kind of always wanted to learn. I took courses. I got the high school diploma here in this country. And I took classes for credit at Santa Monica College. But I decided it was too late in life to go for a degree, and my life was busy raising the family. And now that I have more time, I took up art-- fine art-- painting. And I've been taking classes. And I've been studying at the Beverly Hills senior-- oh, not the senior-- Beverly Hills adult school for eight years now. I also took Conversationally Speaking at the Beverly Hills High School education. And I was looking for something to learn. And it seems that art is the thing for me. I kind of-- since one has to concentrate and focus on the subject one paints completely, it serves for me as therapy, as well as something to learn and create.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we are going to, I think, take a break here and meet your family that's here, and even take a look at some of the art as well as some of your pictures-- if that's all right with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Sally, would you introduce us to your family here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my husband, Steve, my granddaughter Nicole, my daughter Rosalyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their last names-- Nicole and Rosalyn's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Patchi.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Sally, why-- why did you decide to tell your story again? You-- you did this once before for another project, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not exactly the same, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I did it for now. I decided to do this story, even though it's still going to be very, very hurtful. And my dreams-- nightmares-- may come back. But I feel it's my obligation for posterity to tell my story that these things really happened. When I'm gone, the future generations will learn from it. Also, if during my lifetime, they are saying that the Holocaust did not happen-- at least some people say it-- it's my sacred duty to tell my story of my entire family. Also, I would like to tell my story, because of the fact that I feel that people were not as interested in helping us and to save us from the atrocities of Hitler. I would like-- I still cannot comprehend that I've gone through this and cannot comprehend that a nation like Germany has been indoctrinated to the extent, and performed such atrocities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, 1 and 1/2 million Polish people were killed. Does a baby have the choice who it's been born to? A baby has no choice. And I want this to serve as an example that there should be no discrimination against race, religion, or color of the skin. And no one should have to go through what I personally have gone through. Is that something you wrote just to help you? Or is it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a couple of notes, because I knew that I may break down during this interview. And I-- I didn't want my emotions to take over. So I made a couple of notes for myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let me ask you, Rosalyn. Do have anything that you would like to say? Rosalyn, without putting you on the spot-- knowing that you have lots of things zipping through your mind right now-- is there something you'd like to say about the experiences that your mom has or your feelings about them? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's-- yeah, like you said-- lots of things running through my head right now. But one of the most important things I wanted to say was how important it was for my mother to give my sister and me a Jewish education. Also, I just wanted to say how close I am with her. She's like my best friend. I don't know if her experience in the camps-- I don't know if it changed her in any way. I don't know if that was her own personality. But, I mean, she's-- we have such a strong bond-- and also with Nicole, my daughter. She's just such a very loving person. I-- I think maybe it has something to do with the fact that-- to her, life is just-- she knows how precious it is. She's seen, you know, so many, you know, members of her family disappearing. And I also wanted to say how her experiences have haunted me, especially when I was a teenager. Being the same age that my mother was in the camps-- around 13, 14-- I-- I read so many different books on the subject and a lot of autobiographies, and whatnot, because I-- I wanted to try to feel what she felt. I wanted to try to be there to know what she went through. And of course, that was impossible. But I guess as a second generation, I think we probably all go through that, especially when we have such a close relationship with our parents. That's about it. I obviously will be teaching my daughter slowly the way my daughter-- my mother taught me what happened to her and her family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you heard her whole story before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I always used to ask her questions. That was something-- I mean, it was fascinating to me, because it was-- it's just so unbelievable. I always asked her questions, and I asked for details. And, you know, as hard as it was for her to tell me, she was very open with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wrote a paper in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I did a lot of projects about the Holocaust-- papers, and research studies, and whatnot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Well, now thanks to her effort this afternoon for the Shoah Foundation, you and Nicole can watch this together and learn firsthand about grandma's experiences. And we're going to stop now and take a look at the pictures so that we have the complete story, OK? Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I misspoke. Nicole, you had something that you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's-- I just wanted to say one thing-- that I'm really happy that my mom survived-- my grandma survived in this, because if she didn't survive, I wouldn't really have a grandma. And it's really nice that I have a grandma here with me, because if I didn't, I wouldn't have a lot of fun in my life. But since I have my grandma, I really like my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are my two older brothers. Those are reproductions of the pictures we smuggled through in Bergen-Belsen. We hid them in our socks. On the left is my brother, Eliezar; on the right, my brother Hersh Mayer. They perished during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their last names? What are their last names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eliezar-- their last names? Zselinsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were they in those pictures? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were most likely 20 and 23.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where they were taken-- on what occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the last ones to stay behind at the ghetto Srodula, Sosnowiec . And we were all taken away to camps and selections. And we heard later that they stayed until the end-- until judenrein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me on the left with my brother. I kind of look emaciated. It was a couple of months after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the names of the people in the picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's Sally Roisman, nee Zselinsky, and Nathan Zselinsky, my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where that picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Germany a-- a few months after the English liberated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left, this is me, Sally Roisman, my sister Manya-- Mary-- my sister, [NON-ENGLISH], and my brother Simon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are their-- what is their last name at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, we were all Zselinsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken somewhere in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most likely late '40s.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is the man who came into my life in 1957. I met him in New York. His name is Steve Roisman. And we married-- he was born in Hartford, Connecticut. And we married June 13, 1959.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in New York. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From the left is my daughter Rosalyn, my husband Steve is holding our second daughter. And then it's, I, Sally Roisman, with our old-- firstborn, Helen Roisman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Melbourne, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year that might be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was '64, because Roslyn was born in '63.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Going from left to right is my younger daughter, Rosalyn. On the top is my older daughter, Helen, then I, Sally Roisman, and my husband, Steve Roisman

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what the occasion was for this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just wanted to have a family picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably-- judging by the age, Helen must have been 14 years of age and Roslyn, 12. It must have been '74.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- one of my paintings which I did a couple of years ago. And this painting is a la Chagall. And it-- I received an award at Greta Peck's house for this painting. I won second prize.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the one above it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one above it was my first award, was which I did. Somebody sent us. And this was my first award.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long have you been painting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight years now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take you to win your first award, Sally?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took me about four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure you'll get many more awards down the road.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I have one more. This is my latest award, which I received from the Pacific Art Guild. I received it last Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a-- I call it,

speaker: interviewer
text: Very nice. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
